footer.tagline: A quiet place for the people who matter. Made with care for iOS 26.

index.title: Weft — A Calm Personal CRM for iOS · Stay Close to the People Who Matter
index.description: Weft is a quiet, private personal CRM for iOS. Remember friends, family, and the 5–25 people who matter most — on-device, no cloud, no AI, no streaks. A calm alternative to Dex, Clay, and Folk.

hero.titleLead: A quiet place for the people
hero.sub: Weft is a small, private notebook for the five to twenty-five people you actually care about. Open it, see who's on your mind, write a sentence about your last conversation, close. Two seconds. The app remembers what you can't.

phone.title:
Who's on your
mind today?
phone.sarahReason: It's been three weeks. Her mom's surgery was on the 14th — worth asking how it went.
phone.davidReason: He starts the new job Monday. You meant to wish him luck.
phone.momReason: A short call would be nice. Last note: she picked up watercolor again.

manifesto.p1: The people in your life — the five, ten, twenty who actually matter — deserve more than a follow-up reminder. They deserve to be remembered with care: the small things you noticed, the worries they shared, the surgery on the 14th, the new job on Monday, the book they couldn't put down.
manifesto.p2: Weft holds those notes for you. Quietly. On your phone. Without ads, without leaderboards, without asking you to invite three friends.

glance1.p: Today surfaces no more than three names — the people whose rhythm has lapsed. Each card carries the reason: an open thread you left, a date you wanted to remember, a follow-up you said yes to.
glance1.pull: "If everyone's been seen recently, Weft says nothing pressing. Enjoy your day."

glance2.p1: The note sheet takes one breath to open and one to close. Type the thing you'll wish you remembered next month. Toggle Follow up if it should come back as a thread.
glance2.p2: Markdown works invisibly. Photos attach with one tap. No prompts, no fields, no required tags.

phone.title:
Who's on your
mind today?
phone.noteBody: Coffee at Verve. She's been worried about her mom's surgery on the 14th. Started a new book on grief.

glance3.p1: Anything you marked Follow up re-surfaces on its date. Coffee plans, a book recommendation, a job interview you should ask about — held quietly until it's time, then folded into Today as a soft reason.
glance3.p2: Resolve a thread by writing the next note. The chain closes itself.

phone.title: Threads
phone.body1: Ask how her mom's surgery went.
phone.body2: Send her the grief book title — Joan Didion.

negation.intro: It's easier to keep an app calm by being honest about everything it chose not to be. A short list of nos, so you know what you're holding.

crm.p: No pipelines, no stages, no lead status. The market already has fifty of those — and they all feel like work.

contact.p: Apple Contacts already holds phone numbers and addresses. Weft holds memory — the things you'd otherwise forget.

checklist.p: No little flame icons, no chains to keep alive, no guilt when you skip a week. Friendship isn't a workout plan.

journalOfYou.p: Day One already exists for that, and it's wonderful. Weft is a journal about other people, written from your side.

ai.p: No cloud LLM ever sees your notes. No auto-generated reminders. The whole point is that you wrote it.

web.p: iOS only, with a small Watch app, a widget, and an iPad layout. We chose depth over reach — Android can wait.

features.intro: Everything Weft ships is here because it earned its place. Nothing else.

rhythm.p: Pick weekly, biweekly, monthly, or quarterly for each person. The pace bends to the relationship — not the other way around.

threads.p: Mark a thought as "follow up" and it comes back on its date, folded softly into Today. No checkbox guilt.

icloud.p: Off by default. Switch it on for end-to-end-encrypted sync across iPhone, iPad, and Watch — never anyone else's server.

watch.p: A small Watch face shows the one or two people on your mind. A tap caught-them-up; no scrolling, no chrome.

export.p: One tap to PDF or Markdown. Your words are yours; the app is just a place to keep them. Leaving should never be sticky.

ios26.p: Native Liquid Glass, Dynamic Type, Apple Watch, lock-screen widgets, and Apple Shortcuts. Feels like part of the OS, because it is.

pricing.intro: Free for up to seven people on one device. Lifetime is $39.99 — less than two years of yearly, then never again.

free.name: Free
free.f2: Notes, threads, and a daily Today

lifetime.name: Lifetime
lifetime.note: Pay once. Yours forever. No renewals.

data.q: Is my data ever sent to a server?
data.a: By default, no. Weft writes to your phone's local database — that's it. If you switch on iCloud sync, Apple's private database encrypts each note end-to-end with your iCloud key. Weft has no servers, no analytics endpoint, and no third-party SDKs that phone home.

ai.a: Because the value is in you writing the sentence. An AI that auto-summarizes your last text exchange may be technically possible, but it'd hollow the app out. v2 may quietly use on-device Apple Intelligence for tiny things — never to write notes for you.

platforms.q: Will there be an Android version? A web version?
platforms.a: Not for a long time. Weft is built around iOS 26 features — Liquid Glass, Apple Watch, widgets, Apple Shortcuts. Cross-platform would water it down. If you live on Android, we'd rather you use a different app than a worse Weft.

stopPaying.q: What happens if I stop paying?
stopPaying.a: Your data stays. The app reverts to the free tier limits — seven people visible, sync paused — but you keep every note ever written, and you can export the lot to PDF or Markdown with one tap. Subscriptions buy features, not your words.

who.a: One developer, working out of Tokyo. Weft is a deliberate, single-person project — the kind of app you can email about and hear back from the same person who wrote the code. Say hello.

import.q: What about import from Notion / Google Contacts / a CSV?
import.a: v1 imports from Apple Contacts only, and only the names you pick. CSV / Notion / Google import are on the maybe pile for v1.1 — but Weft is intentionally small, and most people who try to import a thousand contacts realize halfway in that they meant fifteen.

gift.q: I received a Weft gift code — how do I use it?
gift.a: Open Weft, tap the gear icon to open Settings, then Redeem a gift code. (Or on the paywall: Have a code? Redeem.) Paste your code into Apple's redemption sheet — if it's a Lifetime gift, Premium activates immediately. No account, no signup. Gift codes come from us directly to people we want to hand the app to (press, friends, occasional giveaways); we don't sell them through the site.

shortcuts.q: Does Weft work with Siri and Shortcuts?
shortcuts.a: Yes — three native actions ship out of the box: Add a Weft note, Open a person in Weft, and Who's on my mind today. Run them from the Shortcuts app, the Action Button, lock-screen widgets, Spotlight search, or just by saying "Hey Siri…". No setup; they appear automatically the first time you launch Weft. All three are free.

cta.p: Free for up to seven. Forever. No account, no card, no email.

learn.h2: What's a personal CRM, and why a quiet one?
learn.intro: A short explainer for everyone who arrived here searching for a relationship app and wondered if Weft was the right fit.

what.p1: A personal CRM is software for people, not for sales pipelines. Where a workplace CRM (Salesforce, HubSpot) tracks leads and deals through a funnel, a personal CRM is a small private space for remembering the humans in your own life — birthdays, last conversations, kids' names, the worry someone mentioned in passing.
what.p2: The category emerged because contact apps are too thin (just phone numbers) and journals are too self-focused (a diary about you, not them). A personal CRM holds the middle ground: per-person memory, gentle rhythm, no public profile.

who.intro: Weft was built for people who want to stay close to a small, defined circle — not network with thousands. If any of these sound like you, you're the audience:
who.l1: The senior engineer who keeps forgetting their coworker's kid's name and feels bad each time.
who.l2: The new parent watching close friendships erode under the weight of admin and exhaustion.
who.l3: The freelancer who wants to remember which client said what, without standing up a CRM.
who.l4: The introvert who loves a small handful of people and wants a private place to think about them.
who.l5: The adult child who lives far from family and wants to call their mom on the right week, not the wrong month.
who.l6: Anyone who tried Notion templates or a spreadsheet and bounced off the overhead.

vs.h: How Weft differs from Dex, Clay, Folk, and other personal CRMs
vs.p1: The personal CRM market splits into two patterns. Networker tools like Dex and Clay are designed for thousands of contacts, LinkedIn-style enrichment, and broadcast follow-ups — useful if you're a founder building a Rolodex, less so if you just want to remember your closest twenty people. Workplace CRM spillover like Folk CRM repackages sales pipelines for personal use; pipelines feel like work even when relabeled.
vs.p2: Weft sits in a third lane. The cap is intentional (five to twenty-five people). The vocabulary is borrowed from journaling, not from sales — notes, threads, rhythm — never "lead," "stage," "engagement." Nothing is shared, nothing is public, nothing is auto-summarized by AI. The whole product is sized around a single idea: that the small circle deserves a calmer, more private surface than the networker apps offer.
vs.p3: If you want auto-pulled contact feeds, LinkedIn integration, or shared workspaces, those products are excellent at it. If you want a quiet, on-device notebook that remembers what you can't, that's Weft.

platform.p1: Weft is built for iOS 26 specifically — native Liquid Glass surfaces, Apple Watch face, lock-screen widgets, Apple Shortcuts, end-to-end-encrypted CloudKit sync. We chose depth over reach: an app that feels like part of the operating system instead of a cross-platform shell that feels like part of none.
platform.p2: Practically: no Android, no web app, no desktop client. Your private notes stay in your iCloud private database, never on a Weft server (because there is no Weft server).

privacy.title: Privacy Policy — Weft Personal CRM for iOS
privacy.description: Weft's privacy policy. On-device by default. End-to-end encrypted iCloud sync is opt-in. No analytics, no third-party SDKs, no AI training on your notes — ever.
privacy.intro: The short version: Weft writes to your phone. It doesn't write to us. If you turn on iCloud, your notes go to Apple — and even Apple can't read them.
privacy.summary: One paragraph summary. Weft has no servers. We do not collect, store, or transmit any data about you or the people in your notes. The app runs entirely on your device. iCloud sync, if you enable it, uses Apple's end-to-end encrypted private database — Weft never sees the contents.

collect.p1: Nothing on our side. Weft has no analytics SDK, no crash-reporting service, no advertising identifiers, and no third-party telemetry of any kind.
collect.p2: The data you create — names, notes, threads, photos you attach, rhythm settings — lives in a SwiftData store on your device's local file system. Standard iOS sandboxing applies: no other app can read it.

icloud.p1: If you toggle iCloud sync on, Weft writes to your iCloud private database. Apple encrypts this with your iCloud key. Apple does not read it; neither do we. We have no way to access it because we have no servers in the loop.
icloud.p2: If you sign out of iCloud, Apple removes the local copy of synced data and our app falls back to its local SwiftData store on that device.

apple.p1: If you bought a subscription, Apple processes the payment under their privacy policy. We see a tokenized identifier saying "this device has an active Premium subscription." We do not see your name, email, billing address, or card number.
apple.p2: The App Store also publishes aggregate, anonymized download counts on our App Store Connect dashboard. We can see "X people in Japan installed Weft this week" — never who, never which devices.

items: No advertising — anywhere, ever. Weft does not sell ads or share data with ad networks.
No selling, renting, or licensing data — to anyone, for any purpose.
No "anonymized analytics" sent to a third-party service.
No AI model is trained on your notes. Weft v1 has no AI, and v2 will only use on-device Apple Intelligence — which stays on your phone.
No public profile, no social graph, no leaderboard.

control.p1: You can export every note to PDF or Markdown with one tap from Settings → Data. Leaving Weft should never be sticky.
control.p2: You can also tap "Delete all data" in Settings to wipe the local store and disable sync. This is irreversible and we can't help you recover anything — because we never had it.

children.p: Weft is rated 4+ in the App Store, but the app's purpose — keeping notes about people in your life — is intended for users 13 years and older. We don't knowingly collect data from anyone, so there's nothing to delete if a younger user installs the app.

changes.p: If we change anything, we'll update the date at the top and note what changed in the app's release notes. We won't expand data collection without a clear, opt-in change you can refuse.

contact.p: Questions or worries? Write to hello@getweft.xyz. A real person — the developer — reads every email.

terms.title: Terms of Service — Weft Personal CRM for iOS
terms.description: Weft's terms of service in plain English. Short, fair, and free of dark patterns. $0 free tier, $18.99/year Premium or $39.99 lifetime via Apple. Refunds through App Store.
terms.intro: You bought an app from a small studio. Here's what that means.
terms.summary: One paragraph summary. Use Weft for its intended purpose — keeping personal notes about people in your life. Don't reverse-engineer, resell, or use it to harass anyone. Your subscription renews until you cancel. Refunds go through Apple. The app is provided as-is.

license.p: When you install Weft, we grant you a personal, non-exclusive, non-transferable license to use the app on iOS devices you control, for as long as the App Store agreement remains in force. You don't own the software; you have permission to use it.

agreeNot.h: What you agree not to do
items: Don't redistribute, sublicense, or resell the app.
Don't reverse-engineer, decompile, or extract source code (except where Apple's standard developer terms or local law explicitly allow).
Don't use Weft to store information about people in a way that's illegal where you live — stalking, harassment, doxxing. Weft is a memory tool, not a surveillance tool.
Don't try to break the paywall. If you find a bug, we'd rather you tell us and we'll buy you a coffee.

subscriptions.pricing: Pricing. Free tier is free forever. Premium is $2.99/month, $18.99/year, or $39.99 once for a Lifetime purchase. The yearly plan includes a 7-day free trial; the monthly and lifetime plans do not.
subscriptions.renewal: Renewal. Subscriptions auto-renew through your Apple ID until you cancel. Apple takes payment 24 hours before the period ends. You can cancel anytime in iOS Settings → your name → Subscriptions, and the cancellation takes effect at the end of the current period.
subscriptions.refunds: Refunds. Refunds are handled by Apple under their refund policy. We can't issue them directly. If you feel you've been treated unfairly by an automatic charge, write to us — we'll help you file the Apple refund request.
subscriptions.priceChanges: Price changes. If we ever raise the price, your existing subscription stays at its current price until you cancel. New subscribers pay the new price. Apple notifies you of any change before charging.

data.p1: You own everything you write into Weft. The notes, names, threads, and photos are yours. We don't claim a license to your content — see the Privacy page for the full picture.
data.p2: You can export everything to PDF or Markdown with one tap and delete the app without warning. We won't email you to come back.

asIs.p: We work hard to make Weft reliable, but software has bugs and devices have rough days. Weft is provided "as is" without warranty. To the extent allowed by law, we're not liable for damages caused by using or being unable to use the app. If something serious goes wrong, please tell us — we'll do what's right by you.

appleEula.p: Apple requires app makers to either provide their own EULA or rely on Apple's standard licensed-application agreement. Where these terms are silent or contradictory, Apple's standard EULA fills the gap.

changes.p: If we change these terms, we'll update the date above. Material changes (anything that affects your rights) will be flagged in the app's release notes. Continued use after a change means you accept the new terms.

law.p: These terms are governed by the laws of Japan, where the studio is based. If you're a consumer somewhere else, mandatory consumer-protection laws in your country still apply to you.

contact.p: Questions, complaints, or legal notices: hello@getweft.xyz. Replies usually within a day or two.

feedback.title: Send Feedback — Weft Personal CRM
feedback.description: Email the developer directly. Bug reports, kind words, refund help — a real person reads every message. No support tickets, no chatbots.
feedback.intro: Something's off? Something's lovely? Something you wish Weft did, or never did? Pick a thread and write — a real human reads every line.

form.emailHint: We won't add you to a list. There is no list.
form.messagePlaceholder: Write whatever you'd say if we were having coffee.
form.footer: Or email hello@getweft.xyz directly. Replies usually within a day or two — sometimes faster.

before.intro: A short list of things people ask. If your question's here, you might not need to wait for a reply.
before.refund: Can I get a refund? Apple handles subscription refunds. Tap your name in iOS Settings → Subscriptions → Weft → Cancel, then request a refund through reportaproblem.apple.com. If Apple says no and you feel it's unfair, write to us and we'll back you up.
before.lost: Lost your notes? If you had iCloud sync enabled, sign in with the same Apple ID on a fresh install and the data will sync back. If you turned sync off and deleted the app, the local data is gone — and we genuinely cannot recover it. We never had it.

featureRequests.title: Feature Requests + Roadmap — Weft Personal CRM
featureRequests.description: What's shipping next in Weft, what's under consideration, and what's been shelved. Submit your own idea — every request reaches the developer.
featureRequests.intro: Weft is built like a careful weekend studio, not a feature factory. We ship one thing at a time, slowly. But the right suggestion can move a quarter.

shipping.label: Shipping next
items: · Birthday-aware Today cards
· Voice notes (transcribed on-device)
· Linked notes between people

considering.label: Considering
items: · iPad split-view layout
· Mac (Catalyst or native)
· On-device Apple Intelligence prompts
· Calendar peek for "last seen"

shelved.label: Shelved
items: · Auto-message your friends
· AI write-the-note-for-me
· Daily affirmations
· Social / shared lists

form.titlePlaceholder: e.g. snooze a person for the holiday season
form.whyPlaceholder: The moment you wished Weft could do this — what were you trying to do?
form.whyHint: Stories are more useful than feature names. Tell us the small thing that prompted the idea.
form.emailPlaceholder: If you'd like us to write back
form.footer: Or read the longer non-goals first — a short list of things Weft has chosen not to be.

about.title: About the Developer — Weft Personal CRM for iOS
about.description: Weft is a one-person indie iOS app made in Tokyo. The why behind the design, the principles, the colophon. Single-developer project, made with care.
about.intro: Weft is a deliberate, single-developer project — the kind of app where the person who wrote the code also answers your email.

why.p1: A few years ago I realized I was losing the small things about the people I cared about most. The name of my friend's new dog. The date a coworker's mother went into surgery. A book one of my closest friends mentioned in passing. These things mattered when I heard them — and then they slipped.
why.p2: I tried the personal CRMs. They felt like Salesforce for friendship: pipelines, stages, follow-up scores. It was the opposite of what I wanted. I wanted a notebook that would remember for me, quietly, without ever asking me to perform care.
why.p3: Weft is that notebook. The five to twenty-five people who matter most, a single sentence a week, on your phone, never anyone else's server.

items: Calm over productive. If a feature would make Weft feel busier, it doesn't get built.
Content over chrome. Your notes are the heroes. The app should mostly disappear.
Apple-first. Native iOS, Liquid Glass, Apple Watch, Shortcuts. We build for one platform and we build it deep.
Local-first. Your data stays on your phone. iCloud only if you ask.
One person at a time. No team workspaces, no shared lists, no social graph. Friendship is private.
Honest pricing. Subscription is fair, free tier is real, exports are forever.

sec.callout: If you want the deeper version, the studio publishes its design language, non-goals, and stack rationale openly in the repo. There are no secrets here — a small app doesn't need them.

where.p: Email is the best channel: hello@getweft.xyz. I usually reply within a day or two. If you'd rather write something publicly, the feature request form is read every Sunday.

colophon.p1: Written in Swift 6. SwiftUI, SwiftData, CloudKit private database, StoreKit 2, Apple WatchKit. Built and tested in Xcode on a single MacBook Pro.
colophon.p2: Type: New York (Apple's serif) and SF Pro. Web fallback: Newsreader by Production Type. Color is sage on aged paper. The arc in the icon is meant to suggest connection without being literal about it — no hearts, no chat bubbles, no people.
